medications.indication_rationale: Topical anaesthetic applied before drinking numbs the ulcers and enables oral fluid intake — the key intervention to prevent dehydration.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply to affected areas ~10 minutes before drinking/eating as directed (adult)

medications.drug_name: topical corticosteroid for oral ulcers (e.g. triamcinolone dental paste)
medications.indication_rationale: Reduces the inflammatory ulcer flare and speeds healing in mucocutaneous Behcet's.
Systemic therapy is reserved for frequent/severe or systemic Behcet's and is a specialist decision, not needed for a single mild mucocutaneous flare.
Acidic drinks worsen ulcer pain; advise cool water/milk instead.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the numbing gel ~10 minutes before sipping cool water or milk, aim for small frequent sips, and track urine output overnight.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Links analgesia to fluid intake to actively reverse early dehydration and monitor response.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Ask a friend to check in tonight given living alone.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Provides a safety check in case symptoms worsen overnight.

patient_education_points.point: These are a flare of your recurrent mouth ulcers (part of a mild autoimmune condition). They're benign and usually heal within 7-10 days, but the pain is stopping you drinking, which is the thing we need to fix tonight.
Use the numbing gel about 10 minutes before drinking cool water or milk (avoid acidic drinks), and take small frequent sips. Keep an eye on how much you're passing urine.
Get face-to-face help tonight (urgent care/ED) if you can't keep fluids down at all, stop passing urine, feel faint/near-collapse, or develop a fever, eye pain, or genital ulcers.

follow_up_plan.interval: reassess tonight after fluid trial; GP within 3-5 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Whether oral rehydration succeeded; ulcer healing; consider referral if flares are frequent/severe.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Unable to maintain fluids, no urine output, worsening dizziness/faintness, fever, or any eye/genital/neurological symptoms.

must_include_items: Objective remote assessment of dehydration severity (urine output, postural symptoms, saliva swallowing)
Topical anaesthetic ± corticosteroid to enable an oral-fluid trial
Explicit dehydration safety-net with same-day face-to-face escalation (noting lives alone)
acceptable_alternatives: Direct same-day face-to-face assessment if the patient cannot swallow saliva or has worsening postural symptoms
Asking a friend/neighbour to check in
errors_of_omission: No topical analgesia to enable drinking
errors_of_commission: Immediately dispatching an ambulance/ED for IV fluids without a hydration assessment or analgesia trial
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: T2/T3 case: passing requires enabling oral rehydration with topical analgesia PLUS a clear dehydration safety-net given the patient lives alone. Failing to assess hydration, or blanket ED dispatch without assessment, are both failures.
